text:
Department of Neurology 

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025 

Dr Aisha Rahman  
St Thomas’ Hospital  
Department of Neurology, Westminster Bridge Road, London SE1 7EH  

Dear Dr Aisha Rahman,  

Michael Turner, DOB: 14-02-1990, Hospital No: L937521 NHS No. 9064157823  
12 Brookside Close, London, SE15 3JP 

Diagnoses: 

- Combined generalised and focal epilepsy (patient has both generalised and focal epilepsy)  
- MRI brain (March 2024): left mesial temporal sclerosis  
- EEG (April 2024): generalised spike–wave bursts with independent left temporal sharp waves  

Current Medication: 

- Levetiracetam 1,500 mg twice daily  
- Lamotrigine 100 mg twice daily  
- Rescue: Buccal midazolam 10 mg as per care plan  

Plan: 

- Urgent admission today for stabilisation in view of nineteen episode of status epilepticus in the past week and inadequate response to current regimen  
- Commence sodium valproate loading and maintenance if no contraindications; continue levetiracetam and lamotrigine pending inpatient review  
- Optimise rescue plan and provide written safety leaflet; liaise with community epilepsy specialist nurse  
- Seizure diary app data to be exported to EHR and used to guide treatment adjustments  
- DVLA advice reinforced: must not drive and to notify DVLA  

I reviewed Michael Turner in my clinic today, accompanied by his partner. He uses a seizure diary app which syncs with his wearable; the entries were reviewed during the consultation. Over the last seven days the diary documents nineteen episode of status epilepticus in the past week, each defined in the app by continuous convulsive activity exceeding five minutes or repeated focal-to-bilateral tonic–clonic events without full recovery. Several events began with a focal impaired-awareness semiology (right facial twitching, speech arrest, manual automatisms) progressing to generalised tonic–clonic seizures. There were also brief absence-like events recorded on two mornings. Triggers noted by the patient include sleep deprivation and a recent viral illness. Medication adherence is reported as high, with app reminders confirming on-time dosing for the past month. Buccal midazolam was administered three times at home per the rescue plan, with partial effect.

This pattern is consistent with combined generalised and focal epilepsy, supported by prior EEG findings of generalised spike–wave activity alongside left temporal sharp waves, and MRI evidence of left mesial temporal sclerosis. Given the recent escalation, breakthrough clusters, and prolonged seizures despite dual therapy, I consider him unstable for outpatient management and have arranged urgent admission via the Same Day Emergency Care pathway for monitoring, intravenous therapy as needed, anti-seizure medication optimisation, and review of precipitating factors (infection screen, metabolic profile, and consideration of status protocol). The inpatient team will upload the last 30 days of seizure diary app logs to the record to assist titration and timing of dosing.

We discussed safety: supervision when bathing, avoiding heights and open water, adherence to the rescue protocol, and ensuring midazolam is in-date and immediately accessible. I advised strict sleep hygiene and infection management. Driving restrictions were explained; he understands that he must not drive and must inform the DVLA. Occupational and social support will be coordinated through the epilepsy specialist nurse. I will arrange a multidisciplinary review following discharge to consider longer-term options including valproate-based therapy if tolerated, and, depending on response, possible evaluation for epilepsy surgery or neuromodulation given the focal onset component.

I will update you following his inpatient stay. Please contact me if further information is required.

With best wishes,

entities.text: nineteen episode of status epilepticus in the past week
nineteen episode of status epilepticus in the past week

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter explicitly states the patient had nineteen episodes of status epilepticus in the past week. There are also brief absence-like events on two mornings, but their exact count is not numerically specified beyond being plural; therefore, per the rule to select the highest frequency and prioritize explicit numeric expressions, we use the clearly stated nineteen in a week. Convert 'nineteen episode ... in the past week' to the allowed format '19 per week.'
